column11: Briefly describe your methodology and scaffolding components (~100 words).  Include:  (1) Your overall approach (e.g., RL, LLM-based, hybrid),  (2) State information used (raw pixels, parsed state, privileged info),  (3) External tools (web search, calculators, etc.),  (4) Memory systems (vector DB, knowledge graphs, etc.),  (5) Feedback mechanisms (human-in-the-loop, checkpointing, etc.),  (6) Fine-tuning (domain-specific training, game-specific RL, etc.).  This information is required for calculating your Adjusted Performance score with the scaffolding penalty.

LLM-based ReAct scaffold layered over the emulator client. State info combines raw screenshots with parsed state (location, map grid, objectives, NPC/movement memory), so the VLM sees both pixels and structured context. No external tools beyond the emulator/VLM APIs—decisions rely on textual prompts. Memory is maintained in-process via ReAct history plus a lightweight failure ledger (location/button counts) and objectives list; no vector DB. Feedback comes from automatic stuck detection, periodic self-reflection prompts, and checkpointable emulator states; no human-in-the-loop mid-run. No fine-tuning or RL—behavior is shaped entirely through prompt engineering and deterministic scaffolding around the base model.

LLM-based ReAct scaffold that drives mGBA inputs via prompt engineering—no RL or fine-tuning, just structured reasoning loops. State info pairs raw screenshots (for dialogue/menus) with parsed state (map grids, player coords, movement memory, objectives, special directives like “climb stairs” or “set clock”). No external tools beyond emulator+VLM APIs. Memory stays in-process: recent thought/action history, failure counters keyed by location/button, and context-specific directives; no vector DB or KG. Feedback comes from automatic stuck detection (unchanged state after a press), special-directive prompts for sensitive scenes, and periodic self-reflection; no human-in-the-loop mid-run. Zero fine-tuning—behavior is entirely shaped by scaffolding and prompts.

"1. Entirely LLM based 2. I refactored the map display to be better - and also broke milestones down more granularly with conditionals based on phase and location (for example, route 103 milestone is huge, walking all the way north, rival battle, and coming back down to Birch's lab) so I milestoned it.  I also implemented AStar pathing to give the LLMs suggestions about how to get to where it was trying to go. I.E if it wanted to go north to find May, it would AStar to the highest northern free pixel and give the LLM that as a suggested action.   Pretty much I prompted the LLM through each phase and held their hand pretty closely LOL  3. No external tools 4. No memory 5. I created custom scripts to make checkpointing easy - i would run the model with my instructions, once it completed a milestone, i'd save the milestones.json and checkpoint and re-run from that point onwards using a custom script (instead of checkpoint.state since it would save objectives for runs that weren't the ones I wanted to submit)  was just a QOL change to make it super easy to re-run specific phases and optimize for time and prompt etc.  6. No fine tuning "

column5: Seonghun Hong, Hyunyoung Jeong, Hyeokgi Kim, Jaeyoon Jung
column11: We used an LLM-based guidebook with tool calling.   The agent receives detailed objectives and image-based goal guidelines, supported by internal tools such as milestone-level temporary memory, an A* pathfinding tool, and a dynamic-thinking mode for harder reasoning (which can switch a thinking mode on/off for latency).  No external tools (e.g., web search) are used and the agent relies only on the frame image and game state, without ground-truth maps or human feedback.   We also tried imitation learning with frame–action pairs, but it failed, so our final system is LLM-only.

column5: Seonghun Hong, Hyunyoung Jeong, Hyeokgi Kim, Jaeyoon Jung
column11: We built an LLM-based guideline and tool-calling agent that can operate fully autonomously without any data collection or human feedback.  The agent receives milestone-level objectives and image-based goal conditions.  - We provide a detailed milestone guideline that helps the agent to reason how to achieve its current milestone. - The agent's guidebook can include a "reference image" (e.g., a photo of the target building) in addition to text tips. If a reference image exists for the current objective, the agent sends two images to the agent simultaneously. The VLM directly compares these two images, allowing it to perform complex visual navigation tasks.  Also, it can use four tools for perception and action, each designed to generalize across the Pokémon journey. - The MEMORY tool stores milestone-level temporal information, allowing the agent to maintain awareness of its current state. - The PATHFINDING and DIRECTION tools use an A* algorithm to compute efficient navigation paths. - The THINKING tool enables dynamic reasoning effort control, switching on or off depending on task difficulty to control latency.  We do not use any global ground-truth map information, and the agent has no access to external tools such as web search. It relies solely on the frame image and the in-game state. In addition, we don’t use external memory systems like vector DB.  Because no human feedback is used, the system functions as a fully end-to-end autonomous agent. Our method generalizes effectively to Pokémon speedrunning tasks, as it requires no expert demonstrations or behavior-cloning data.   *This is our final submission which contains the whole logs. **We successfully cleared the final milestone(FIRST_GYM_COMPLETE) and even exited the gym, but the milestone did not update in the submission log.  However, for fairness, we did not modify the milestone code, and we have video evidence confirming the result.

Also, it can use four tools for perception and action, each designed to generalize across the Pokémon journey. - The MEMORY tool stores milestone-level temporal information, allowing the agent to maintain awareness of its current state. - The PATHFINDING and DIRECTION tools use an A* algorithm to compute efficient navigation paths. - The THINKING tool enables dynamic reasoning effort control, switching on or off depending on task difficulty to control latency.  We do not use any global ground-truth map information, and the agent has no access to external tools such as web search. It relies solely on the frame image and the in-game state. In addition, we don’t use external memory systems like vector DB.  Because no human feedback is used, the system functions as a fully end-to-end autonomous agent. Our method generalizes effectively to Pokémon speedrunning tasks, as it requires no expert demonstrations or behavior-cloning data.   *This is our final submission which contains the whole logs. **We successfully cleared the final milestone(FIRST_GYM_COMPLETE) and even exited the gym, but the milestone did not update in the submission log.  However, for fairness, we did not modify the milestone code, and we have video evidence confirming the result.

column11: UPDATED: We have also implemented milestone rewards up to the second gym and had started training them. However, we abandoned this approach because it was outside of the scope for the competition and we were under time pressure. However, initial results were extremely promising and we were close to the second badge.  Methodology Description:  (1) Overall Approach: We used a RL approach to solve the problem. For this, we implemented a fast serverless version we could train on. We experimented with recurrent PPO and Dreamer_v3. Due to the constraints of the competition, we decided to  use recurrent PPO. Here is gif of the Dreamer reconstruction: https://imgur.com/a/yEs8hJe . The main problem using the Dreamer approach was an excessive training time. In particular, sample efficiency was not a bottleneck, but training speed was. Thus, if there was any small bug in the code/env/server, we would have to re-run a significant amount of the training. Thus, instead of using 2 expensive H100, we used many cheap L4s with strong CPUs for recurrent PPO. The exact amount varied, since they were, unlike the two H100s, constantly being preempted and thus we never really could use all the VMs we started. In general though, we mostly had >4 L4 GPUs running. The main advantage of Dreamer_v3 would have been to only use visual data and maybe location data. However, since these restrictions were lifted, there was no reason to use Dreamer_v3, even though initial experiments showed promising results.   (2) State Information Used: The RL Agent uses the raw pixels scaled to 128x128x1 (grayscale), a location one-hot encoded vector, and a 0/1 encoded milestone vector which tracks the current milestones achieved. The milestones roughly correspond to the milestones of the competition, but sometimes contain multiple sub-milestones (e.g. talked to team aqua grunt, battle team aqua grunt). We tried using text embeddings for the dialogue, but it did not improve performance measurably.  (3) External Tools:  None.  (4) Memory Systems: A pseudo-memory which tracked the milestones we achieved (see 2). Otherwise, none.  (5) Feedback mechanisms: We used general rewards (such as defeated trainer, heal reward, attack used reward, new coordinate explored reward, …) and specific rewards based on the milestones (pokedex received reward, entered Olddale town rewarded, visiting pokecenter for the first time reward, …). We also frequently checkpointed and then continued training with the same weights or new weights at the sub-milestones/checkpoints. Sometimes we also had to adjust the rewards, as sometimes it figured out how to exploit the rewards.  (6) In some situations we fine-tuned using a termination condition, e.g. if the agent leaves the gym without a badge, the session is immediately terminated. This was to allow for rapid reloads, since otherwise it would be wasting a whole episode doing random actions.  Appendix: Here are some results of Dreamer_v3 reconstruction and imagination using a completely untrained model and a slightly pretrained one (amazingly, even a minor amount of pretraining creates a relatively robust initial world model): Untrained: https://imgur.com/a/DFv76pi Pretrained: https://imgur.com/a/yEs8hJe

column11: (1) Overall Approach: A neural network agent is trained using DAgger with a VLM-based Markovian policy synthesis module. For each milestone, an LLM generates tool-using policy code (in a Voyager-style framework) that provides expert demonstrations for neural policy training. (2) State Information: A VLM extracts state features from raw pixels, including position, objectives, and spatial context. This is combined with privileged memory information from the game, such as coordinates, Pokémon statistics, inventory items, badges, and internal flags. (3) External Tools: The system uses A* pathfinding with collision detection, an automated battle handler with type-effectiveness logic, a UI navigation system, OCR-based dialog detection, NPC avoidance utilities, and milestone-specific tool compositions. (4) Memory Systems: A persistent knowledge base stores over 600 map locations with portal connections. The system includes a milestone-based curriculum, a cache of generated policy code per milestone, and experience replay buffers used for DAgger training. (5) Feedback Mechanisms: Policy generation uses retry logic. On failed attempts, the policy synthesis step receives recent screenshots and execution logs from prior attempts to refine and regenerate the code. (6) Fine-tuning: DAgger (+ RL) training is used to iteratively teach the neural policy from VLM-synthesized expert demonstrations across the milestone curriculum.

<This is a test submission>  LLM Agentic Framework using Hierarchical Framework with a Planner Agent Node which would do the planning necessarily. And the tools can be used by agent. State Information would be feed to the agent when its need to be used.

Hierarchical structure: Before invoking action nodes, the framework checks whether a planning step is needed (e.g., when entering a new location/map, when the agent has been stuck for several steps). Action nodes are organized into three categories—(1) overworld, (2) battle, and (3) other—to provide task‑specific context and specific information that improves execution quality and reduce the hallucination. The planner uses a larger LLM (e.g., GPT‑5) for high‑level reasoning, while the action nodes use a smaller, faster model (e.g., GPT‑4/SLM) for low‑level control — suggesting a promising design pattern of LLM‑for‑planning and SLM‑for‑execution. Tool calling is under development, but the skeleton was built in the framework.

I used an LLM scaffolding based approach. I use a planning agent that receives the main storyline objectives, then creates sub-objectives for the action agent to follow. The planning agent is called whenever all the remaining sub objectives are completed.  I gave the action agent two new tools: navigate_to and interact_with. navigate_to lets the agent navigate to a specific reachable coordinate. To determine the reachable tiles, I run BFS on the json map data on each turn and feed those to the action agent. interact_with does the same thing, but appends an 'A' to the end of the path.

(1) Overall approach It is a graph-based exploration/search algorithm. It builds a graph representation of the game’s map during the run and uses it to explore (see README.md for more details).  (2) State information * (x, y) coordinates * location string (e.g. LITTLEROOT TOWN) * game image (for checking if the game is in dialog only)  (3) Tools The BFS algorithm is used.  (4) Memory A graph of the game's map is constructed from scratch during the run (no information from prior runs)  (5) Feedback No human or automated feedback is used.  (6) Fine-tuning No training was done, and no extra pokemon data was used.  Additional information: * The video is 11 hours long but the actual runtime was less than 6 hours. This is also reflected in submission.log.  * The codebase includes code for RL approaches that was not used in this submission's run.  * The run was generated using the script rl.py rather than run.py. The interaction with the server is done in gymansium_env.py. This was to make it more convenient for our attempts using RL. Our code still uses app.py for the server but does not make use of the function run_multiprocess_client in client.py. The code app-dev.py was not used for this run.

(1) Overall approach It is a graph search + VLM hybrid approach. It builds a graph representation of the game's map during the run and uses it to explore. The VLM determines the current scene (e.g. overworld, menu, dialog, battle) which determines what approach to use. The VLM also handles some individual actions (see README.md for more details).  (2) State information * (x, y) coordinates * location string (e.g. LITTEROOT TOWN) * game image * machine-readable map of the surroundings (from game state)  (3) Tools BFS algorithm  (4) Memory A graph representation of the game's map that is being constructed  (5) Feedback No human or automated feedback  (6) Fine-tuning * No fine-tuning or training * Some game-specific suggestions are given in the prompts for the VLM  The run was executed with the following command: python run.py --backend gemini --model-name gemini-2.5-flash-lite --no-ocr --agent-auto --record

(1) Overall approach It is a graph search + VLM hybrid approach. A graph representation of the game's world is built during the run for navigation. VLM calls high-level actions depending on the scene (e.g. battle, overworld - determined by the VLM), which are converted into buttons using tools. Explore is a key high-level action, which is used to visit new coordinates and talk to NPCs in new locations (destinations determined by graph search).  This run improve from the previous run with a few bug fixes and the use of single movement actions at a time rather than multiple.  (2) State information used * raw pixels * x, y coordinates + location string (e.g. LITTLEROOT TOWN) * machine-readable map of the surroundings (from the game state) * current dialog (from the game state) * team info (from the game state) * player name and money (from the game state)  (3) External tools * BFS algorithm for navigation  (4) Memory system * graph representation of the game's world * history of previous dialog * previous objectives generated by the agent  (5) Feedback mechanisms * no checkpointing or human interventions  (6) Fine-tuning * a pretrained Faster-RCNN object detection model was fine-tuned to detect NPCs/trainers using self-labeled data * template image of the clock in Brendan's room and Bag in Route 101 used to detect them * pre-defined sequences of actions for selecting moves during battle and navigating the menu

column11: We implement an agent inspired by cognitive science / architectures, it is a LLM-based agentic approach. •⁠  ⁠Perception module to parse visual input and fuse with other sources like visual map into structured scenes  •⁠  ⁠Saliency module as an attention mechanism, identifying interruption or invalidation of current plans.  •⁠  ⁠Visual memory with vector retrieval using ChromaDB •⁠  ⁠A long term episodic memory to store past experiences  •⁠  ⁠A short term working memory •⁠  ⁠Planner-executor loop: Planner strategically select goals to delegate to task-specialized executors (navigation, battle, general) We integrated these techniques into our final MVP Hierarchical agent, which utilized all of the above components to provide a deliberate and effective framework.

(3) External tools: None.    (4) Memory: Simple deques for observation/action buffers. No vector DB.    (5) Feedback: Automatic checkpointing (game state + LLM history). No human-in-the-loop. **Approach**: Hybrid VLM + programmatic architecture. Gemini-2.5-flash-lite handles perception and action selection; programmatic state machines ensure reliability for deterministic sequences (opener, battle, navigation).  **State (S)**: Raw pixels via VLM + privileged state (pathfinding, battle mechanics) + OCR fallback for dialogue.  **Tools (T)**: Offline location graph from game wikis; no real-time external dependencies.  **Memory (M)**: Dialogue history (10-message buffer), position tracking, milestone flags, battle state.  **Feedback (F)**: Checkpoint evaluation at splits; no human-in-the-loop.  **Fine-tuning (Φ)**: None

Pokemon Emerald Speedrun Agent - Methodology    Overall Approach: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with LSTM temporal modeling, trained using PufferLib's vectorized environment framework.    Observation Space:   - Visual Input: Screen capture (3 channels: game pixels, visited map mask, local exploration overlay)   - Game State Features: Map ID/bank, player direction, blackout location   - Pokemon Data: Party species/levels/HP/status (6 slots)   - Progression Tracking: Pokedex count, badges, inventory items   - Embeddings: Categorical features encoded (map: 256→8dim, species: 412→16dim, moves: 355→8dim, items: 378→5dim)    Policy Architecture:   - Screen Encoder: 3-layer CNN (3→32→64→64 channels, 8×4→4×2→3×1 kernels)   - Fusion Layer: Concatenate CNN features + embeddings → 512-dim hidden state   - Output: Actor (8 actions) + Critic value head   - Temporal Context: LSTM wrapper (512-dim hidden state)    Model Size:   - Base Policy (CNN + Embeddings): 13.76M parameters   - Total with LSTM wrapper: 15.86M parameters     - CNN: 75.9K     - Embeddings: 19.5K     - Fusion encoder: 13.66M     - Heads: 4.6K     - LSTM: 2.10M    State Representation: CNN processes visual features (26,624-dim output); separate embedding tables encode categorical game state. Flattened fusion layer enables end-to-end training.    External Tools: None (autonomous neural network)    Memory Systems: LSTM maintains temporal context across frames; checkpoint system tracks milestone progression.    Feedback Mechanisms:   - Milestone-based rewards (game progression)   - Swarm curriculum learning (migrate to best checkpoint when any agent achieves new milestone)   - Automatic checkpointing at milestone achievements

column11: My overall approach involved 2 main modifications to the simple.py baseline via the addition of an agent_utils Baseline. 1) I added NPC detection when in the overworld state (the VLM is tasked if returning the relative position of NPCs and 2) defering most navigation to a pathfinding utility. The agent is instructed  to identify where on the formatted_state map it wants to travel to, given its broader objective and add 'REQUEST_PATH: (x, y)' to its response. This response is then processed and if REQUEST_PATH is found in the response the pathfinder utility is called to perform A* search and enqueue a valid sequence of actions (up to a max of 10) to get the agent to said location. If NPC_DETECTION is enabled, this path will also respect the position of NPCs.

This submission completes the first half of the split 02, including everything except the navigation of the agent from Littleroot Town to the rival house to the rival bedroom.  This method uses the CLI agent to interact with the game via native function calling. Pathfinding was deprecated due to bugs in the navigate_to endpoint. The main contribution in this split is the addition of a 'direct objective' module that provides the agent with explicit direction as to what objectives it is on, navigation hints, as well as the complete_direct_objective endpoint to allow the agent to move onto the next objective. While other endpoints for knowledge search and web search exist, the agent was directed to prioritize movement and interaction.

This submission is my second attempt at completing split 03, prompting my agent to select Treeko as a starter for better survivability.  This method uses the CLI agent to interact with the game via native function calling. Pathfinding is now implemented via the navigate_to() endpoint, which automatically converts the agent's request to move to a given walkable coordinate on the current map to a sequence of button actions, abstracting the more difficult problem of long-distance navigation. To accomplish this, the mapping system was overhauled. The scaffold now uses the full Pokémon Emerald map data from Porymap. When the agent enters a new map (as read from the ROM), a lookup function is used to call a parser which (1) formats the full map data for the agent and (2) provides a clean grid for A* search.   Another contribution to this split is the addition of a 'direct objective' module that provides the agent with explicit direction on which objective it is on, navigation hints, and the complete_direct_objective() endpoint to allow the agent to move to the next objective. While other endpoints for knowledge search and web search exist, the agent was directed to prioritise movement and interaction.  Notes: I forgot to add the final objective of returning back to professor birch's lab to receive the pokedex. This is a natural opportunity to extend the capabilities of the agent by allowing it to intelligently create additional objectives, given (1) its prior understanding of Pokemon Emerlad, and several functions that enable it to search the web, pokemon wiki, and read/write to files. I'll look to implement these changes to complete this milestone and the next.

column11: This is my first attempt at completing split 06. The main contribution this split makes is in refactoring the pathfinding system to better respect tile behaviour (i.e ledges are no longer treated as blocked), as well as debugging some minor errors with the navigate_to() endpoint.  A few notes from experimentation: I want to highlight 02:10. Once the agent enters Petalburg Woods, its next objective is to travel to the northern section of Route 104. In its first attempt, it navigates to the southern warp leading to Route 104 and completes its objective, which is incorrect. It is only brought back to the correct path by being served the next objective -- travelling to rustburo city. I will likely deprecate this objective (Petalburg Woods to Route 104 North) to avoid ambiguity in the future.  Agent cannot critique/improve objectives when stuck (i.e requesting a path to an inaccessible warp. Currently, I prompt the agent to try a different warp in this case.  Agent is very sensitive to slight nuances in the naming of objectives. If I have route_104_south as an objective, it tries to navigate to the southern part of route 104 instead of recognising that it has reached the southern section of Route 104. This underscores the importance of well-structured objectives to avoid ambiguity.   NEXT STEPS: Given that the agent's Treeko is running low on health with little PP. It is likely to be sent back to Petalburg City. I will test how effective it is at navigating back to complete its current objective (Rustburo City) and document additional scaffolding as needed.

This submission completes the team aqua grunt battle and successfully navigates to route 104 north. For this submission, I add system instructions to the vertex backend for the CLI agent to disambiguate certain actions like selecting moves correctly and better prompting the agent to not run from battles.

In this split, the agent successfully navigates to Rustboro City, heals its Pokémon, and then attempts to navigate back to the grass patch in Route 104 North to catch some Pokémon. The agent gets stuck in a loop trying to access its bag to catch the zigzagoon owned by the trainer due to an ambiguous objective. The following split will disambiguate this objective to allow the agent to make more meaningful progress.

This split immediately follows the agent's successful catching of Taillow. It then proceeds to the Rustboro gym where it battles the first trainer.  Note: The agent consistently struggles to navigate from the top of Route 104 into Rustboro

In this split, the agent successfully navigates to Roxanne, defeats her, and exits the gym.  A Few notes about this run: I improved the pathfinding system by allowing the agent to pass in a variance parameter to the navigate_to() endpoint from low to extreme. The higher the variance, the more random, non-overlapping steps the final generated path samples over. This is to improve obstacle navigation in very cluttered environments. The objective system for automatic objective creation (on the part of the agent) is not fully complete, but towards the end of the run, we see that the agent generates the next few steps and attempts to complete them.
